title: Systemd units for networks
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

While systemd units allow for full portability of pods and containers to another system, when pods are run with `--network`, the specified network must exist first, otherwise it is not possible to start the pod unit.  
Is it possible to export network configurations as systemd units so they can be started before pods requiring them, for instance when restoring all the networks, pods and containers to another server?

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

```bash
podman network create testnet
podman pod create --name testpod --network testnet
podman generate systemd --new --name --files testpod
podman network rm --force testnet
systemctl --user start pod-testpod
```

**Describe the results you received:**

```
Feb 27 14:40:24 host systemd[438]: Starting Podman pod-testpod.service...                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Feb 27 14:40:24 host podman[509270]: Error: unable to find network with name or ID testnet: network not found                                                                                                                                                                             
Feb 27 14:40:24 host systemd[438]: pod-testpod.service: Control process exited, code=exited, status=125/n/a                                                                                                                                                                               
Feb 27 14:40:24 host podman[509280]: Error: name or ID cannot be empty                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Feb 27 14:40:24 host systemd[438]: pod-testpod.service: Control process exited, code=exited, status=125/n/a                                                                                                                                                                               
Feb 27 14:40:24 host systemd[438]: pod-testpod.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.                                                                                                                                                                                                   
Feb 27 14:40:24 host systemd[438]: Failed to start Podman pod-testpod.service.                                                                                                                                                                                                            
Feb 27 14:40:24 host systemd[438]: pod-testpod.service: Scheduled restart job, restart counter is at 1.                                                                                                                                                                                   
Feb 27 14:40:24 host systemd[438]: Stopped Podman pod-testpod.service.
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

`podman generate systemd` also creates a `network-testnet.service` unit file, and `pod-testpod.service` requires `network-testnet.service` to start first (I guess it would require implementing a `--replace` flag as for pods and containers)

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.1
API Version:  4.0.1
Go Version:   go1.17.7
Git Commit:   c8b9a2e3ec3630e9172499e15205c11b823c8107
Built:        Sun Feb 27 14:21:56 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
not relevant
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
not relevant
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

not relevant

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @AlphaJack.

> Is it possible to export network configurations as systemd units so they can be started before pods requiring them, for instance when restoring all the networks, pods and containers to another server?

At the time of writing, this is not possible.

I see the use case of creating networks on the fly but there is a number of obstacles:
* What if the network already exists?
* Should the previous one be deleted/recreated or used?
* Should the network be removed once the container exits?

I don't think we'll find a one-size-fits-all solution.

Users are free to customize the generated units though.  In this case, for instance, it may work to add an `ExecStartPre=podman network create` and an `ExecPostStop=podman network rm`.

The commands needed to create networks should be pretty easy to reconstruct, so we could probably add network creation logic to a `ExecStartPre` in unit files from `podman generate systemd --new`. We'd need a `podman network create --replace` (or maybe `podman network create --if-not-exist` or similar?) but otherwise it seems simple.

Food for thought, I have a dozen pods that run on a custom network. It makes me queasy to think there'd be so many unit files with identical `ExecStartPre podman network create` statements 😁 If networks had their own systemd unit file, it would reduce duplication and let systemd manage the dependency to start and stop when necessary. And it would simplify the case where multiple networks are involved.

> The commands needed to create networks should be pretty easy to reconstruct

No it's not. There are many corner cases.
First, we do not know if --subnet was given or automatically assigned. So we do not know if we have to add --subnet to the command.
Second, we support two network backends. A config might work for only one backend so it can fail if the system uses the other backend.
For cni it is impossible to reconstruct the config when a custom config is used.
For macvlan configs the interface name might not exists on the other system.

Also I agree with @vrothberg points.

Would it make sense to add an option to generate the the network unit file
podman generate systemd --add-network ...

Or something like that.

I am torn.  I understand it would be helpful in this specific case but also we need to draw a line somewhere.  Would we do the same for volumes?

> Would it make sense to add an option to generate the the network unit file
podman generate systemd --add-network ...

This could be a nice middle ground. Personally, I'd prefer something in `ExecStartPre` and extend `network create` to only create the network if it doesn't exist.
Volumes auto-create if they do not exist, so I think that's somewhat of a different matter.

> I'd prefer something in ExecStartPre

As I mentioned earlier, I have (so far) a dozen pods running, so 11 of the `ExecStartPre` would be no-ops 😁 Presumably, they'd all be no-ops after the first run or would the network be deleted in `ExecStopPost` after the last container stops? And what a mess if I add another network 😁

Adding an option does not fix the underlying problems mentioned in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13365#issuecomment-1054017279 or https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13365#issuecomment-1054633148

I don't think there is a good solution here.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we see no good solution and no feedback in a month, I am going to close.
